channel.title: Posts on ISH.SYS
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on ISH.SYS

item.title: From Prototype to Enterprise Agent Security
item.description:
Open Agent Policy has crossed an important line: it is no longer just a sketch of how agent authorization might work.

The project now has a spec, schemas, conformance tests, a Go evaluator, a server, a Python SDK, LangChain integration, an MCP proxy, an HTTP gateway, sessions, runs, scoped grants, Postgres storage, audit query, OPA and Cedar backend adapters, and a production-like validation environment.

item.title: Protecting Agent Tools: SDK, MCP Proxy, and Gateway
item.description:
Agent authorization is only useful if it sits in the path of real actions.

That sounds obvious, but it is where many security designs get thin. A policy engine that works only in a demo function is not enough. Agents call tools through SDKs, framework abstractions, MCP servers, HTTP APIs, reverse proxies, and custom integration code.

item.title: Sessions, Runs, and Scoped Grants for Agents
item.description:
An authorization decision is useful, but it is not the whole enforcement story.

Imagine an agent asks the policy server:

Can I read ticket T-100?

The policy server says yes. Now the agent calls the ticket API.

How does the ticket API know the policy decision actually happened? How does it know the decision was for ticket.read, not customer.bulk_export? How does it know the grant was for T-100, not every ticket? How does it know constraints like redaction or record limits should be applied?

item.title: Building Open Agent Policy Spec-First
item.description:
Open Agent Policy is not just a server. It is a contract between agents, SDKs, gateways, proxies, resource APIs, CLIs, and policy engines.

That is why I wanted the project to be spec-first.

If the Python SDK constructs one shape of authorization request, the Go server interprets a slightly different shape, and the MCP proxy invents a third version, the project becomes a pile of adapters instead of a policy framework. Agent authorization is already subtle enough. The wire contract should not be.

item.title: Agent Identity Is Not a String
item.description:
Every agent security design eventually runs into the same trap: it starts with an agent_id.

That seems reasonable. We need to know which agent is acting, so we give it a stable identifier:

agent://support/ticket-assistant

The problem is that a string is only a claim. It tells us what the caller wants to be called. It does not tell us whether the caller is actually that agent.

item.title: Why AI Agents Need a Control Plane
item.description: The more useful an AI agent becomes, the less it looks like a chatbot and the more it looks like a distributed system component. It has tools. It has credentials. It reads tickets, queries databases, sends emails, opens pull requests, calls APIs, and sometimes chains those actions together without a human approving every single step.

item.title: Graph Algorithms: Mathematics & Implementation
item.description:
Graphs are one of the most versatile data structures in computer science. They model everything from social networks to routing protocols to dependency resolution. In this post, we’ll build up graph theory from first principles — formal definitions, mathematical analysis, and clean Python implementations for each algorithm. *This post assumes familiarity with basic data structures (arrays, hash maps, heaps) and Big-O notation.

Foundations

Formal Definition

A graph $G$ is an ordered pair $G = (V, E)$ where:

item.title: Mathematics in Web Publishing
item.description:
One of the challenges of technical writing on the web is properly rendering mathematical notation. With KaTeX, we can render both inline and display math beautifully.

Inline Math

The quadratic formula $x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ is one of the most well-known formulas in mathematics. We can also write Euler’s number $e \approx 2.71828$ inline.

Display Math

Euler’s identity is often cited as the most beautiful equation:

$$e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$$
